notes.note: C. Aquilius Gallus, presiding judge in the case; one of the great Roman jurists, later author of the actio de dolo malo, the standing remedy against fraud.
Roman judges sat with a consilium of advisors, distinguished men selected for the case; their counsel framed the verdict.
The head of a Roman household, with full legal authority over its members and property; its prudence and attentiveness were the prime test of social standing.
An auctioneer; a respectable but distinctly commercial calling, often associated with the Atria Licinia at Rome.

The temple of the Dioscuri in the Forum, used in Cicero's day as a public point for checking treasury account-balances; "asking at Castor's" was the way to learn what was actually being paid out, as distinct from what the books said was owed.
Surety for appearance; a defendant gave a vadimonium binding himself to appear at a stated future date for the next phase of proceedings, on pain of penalty if he did not.

The first day of February. The Romans counted backwards from three named days each month: Kalends (1st), Nones (5th or 7th), and Ides (13th or 15th). "Two days before the Kalends of February" is 29 January, counted inclusively.
The consuls of 83 BC, the year in which the events of the speech took place: L. Cornelius Scipio Asiagenus and C. Norbanus. The Romans named years by their consuls.

Each praetor at the start of his year issued an edict laying out the formulae and remedies he would grant; the framework of Roman civil procedure for the year. Naevius's application was for an order under the edict permitting attachment of an absent debtor's goods.
A money-changer's table or banking spot in the Forum; Naevius staged his "witnessing" of Quinctius's failure to appear here, on a fixed hour to ensure plausibility.
The 13th of September. (The Ides falls on the 15th in March, May, July, October; on the 13th in all other months.)

Tribunes of the plebs (ten elected annually) had the power to forbid (intercessio) the proceedings of any other magistrate at the petition of a citizen; appeals to them are a recurring procedural recourse in Cicero's forensic speeches.

A formal Roman legal wager: the parties agreed in writing that one would pay a sum to the other if a specified proposition was found true. In Pro Quinctio the sponsio is the form by which the substantive question (was Quinctius's property lawfully attached?) is brought to trial.

satis dare iudicatum solvi: to provide sureties undertaking that the judgment, if adverse, will be paid. Quinctius's refusal to give such security would itself be tantamount to a plea of guilty; hence his preference for the alternative form, the sponsio.
